questions.text: I enjoy shaping the culture of any team I join.
My emotional intensity means that I live life in high definition — nothing is blurred or softened.
I calibrate my effort to the importance of each task, giving more attention to high-impact work.
I do not naturally notice when someone is being deliberately vague to maintain deniability.
I treat my calendar as a rough suggestion that I adjust throughout the day.
I feel that self-interest should always yield to the greater good.
I feel that moral seriousness about suffering means being willing to stay engaged with it long after the emotional intensity has faded.
I pick up on when someone is listening to a story not for the story itself but for what it reveals about the storyteller.
I find that I am able to maintain my composure naturally, without having to actively manage my reactions.
I hold the view that groups need a strong voice to stay focused.
I find that I am more interested in the logical structure of an argument than in its social context.
When I'm excited about something, I feel it in every cell of my body.
I find that maintaining composure when confronted with others' pain allows me to consider all sides of a situation without bias.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering is most authentic when it leads to a change in what I do, not just what I feel.
I feel that establishing equal footing early strengthens collaboration later.
I detect when someone is using self-deprecating humor to preemptively manage others' impressions.
I find that I can listen to someone describe an intensely emotional situation without my own system activating.
I hold the view that I should not apologize for wanting the best for myself.
I find that deliberate emotional regulation during exposure to others' pain preserves both my effectiveness and my long-term wellbeing.
I feel that a well-structured routine is essential to productivity.
I believe that openness to others' views is necessary for effective teamwork.
I let the group collectively decide when a project's scope should change.
I am quick to take the microphone, literally or figuratively.
I notice when a person's tone becomes overly measured, as if they are carefully controlling their emotions.
I find that most things that upset other people register as mild inconveniences for me.
I tend to view my savings as a tool for my own future, not a resource for others.
I believe that serving others without expectation of return is a core part of who I am.
I hold the view that moral engagement with suffering is the clearest and most authentic way I have to express what I believe in.
I feel that a commitment made is a debt unpaid until it's fulfilled.
I tend to use windfalls on personal upgrades rather than distributing them to others.
I find that I make better decisions about how to help when I can maintain emotional distance from the pain I am witnessing.
I often do not realize when a social norm has been violated until someone reacts.
I would trade personal recognition for the satisfaction of knowing my efforts lifted the team.
I respond to new information by pivoting quickly rather than updating a formal plan.
I follow my curiosity regardless of my energy level, trusting engagement will sustain me.
When someone shares good news, my own excitement can rival theirs in intensity.
